title: The Turk retires, a stats class levels up, and three tricks that were one
summary: Three unrelated stories: Amazon winds down Mechanical Turk (the hidden-human-behind-the-AI marketplace, now hollowed out by workers who secretly use LLMs), a Dartmouth study where an LLM-graded statistics 'textbook' lifted final-exam scores 0.71-1.30 SD but where the real lever was constructed-response practice, not the chatbot, and a paper proving GRPO, Dr. GRPO, and DAPO are one RL knob — the group's reward standard deviation. Plus a sweep on Microsoft 365 price hikes, a Claude tool-calling gotcha, a 'building to the test' coding-agent benchmark paper, LongCat open weights, the junior-programmer job market, Zuckerberg's admission that Meta's AI agents haven't progressed as hoped, and the Anthropic-Alibaba feud.

sources.title: Balancing Efficacy and Engagement in Interactive Texts (Phosphor, Dartmouth MATH 010)
GRPO, Dr. GRPO, and DAPO Are Three Operations on One Number: The Group-Standard-Deviation Identity
Building to the Test: Coding Agents Deliver What You Check, Not What You Requested
LongCat-2.0 (1.6T total / ~48B active MoE) weights open under MIT license
Amazon will stop accepting new customers for Mechanical Turk (TechCrunch)
Simon Willison: Better Models, Worse Tools (Armin on invented tool fields, incl. Opus 4.8)
Laurie Voss: AI has torched the market for junior programmers
Mark Zuckerberg tells Meta staff AI agents haven't progressed as hoped (TechCrunch)
Alibaba reportedly bans employees from using Claude Code (TechCrunch)

topics: Amazon Mechanical Turk closes to new customers July 30, 2026 (existing customers continue, no new features); launched 2005 as a marketplace for tiny human-done tasks machines couldn't do, marketed as 'artificial artificial intelligence' and named for the 18th-century chess-automaton hoax (a human hidden inside a supposed machine); long the hidden-human enabler of fake-it-till-you-make-it AI; a 2023 analysis (cited by TechCrunch) found 33-46% of Turk workers using LLMs to do the tasks, poisoning it as a source of human ground truth; the broader shift is model-graded/synthetic data replacing the cheap human-in-the-loop, with the loss being the messy distribution-of-real-people signal

Phosphor (Jonah Bard, Dartmouth): an LLM-graded formative-assessment 'textbook' deployed in 3 sections of Intro Statistics (MATH 010), ~150 students, Spring 2026; Claude Sonnet grades constructed-response answers against instructor rubrics; adopted by 90.2%; full-engagement gap on the final exam of 1.30 SD unadjusted / 0.71 SD controlling for midterms (authors call 0.71 a conservative floor); observational, self-selection the central threat; a natural experiment across modules showed the dosage effect held for constructed-response quizzes but vanished for MCQ-only — the testing/doer effect is the mechanism, not the chatbot (RAG chat got just 72 queries all course); the LLM's role is cheap open-ended grading at scale, enabling old, well-established pedagogy

'GRPO, Dr. GRPO, and DAPO Are Three Operations on One Number' (HF Daily Papers): proves the three popular RLVR methods differ only in how they treat the group reward standard deviation; for binary right/wrong rewards sigma = sqrt(k(G-k))/G, and that disagreement IS the size of the training update (the group-standard-deviation identity) — max when a group of samples splits evenly, zero when unanimous ('falls silent'); GRPO divides by sigma, Dr. GRPO drops the division, DAPO discards zero-sigma groups; yields closed forms for samples-per-prompt needed and the silent-group rate; validated on Big-Math and a controlled run

repeat_coverage.story: LongCat-2.0 weights open-sourced under MIT (sweep line)
repeat_coverage.repeat_of: 2026-06-30 open-weights release day (LongCat-2.0 revealed as the stealth 'owl-alpha')
repeat_coverage.new_development: Meituan released the LongCat-2.0 weights openly under an MIT license — previously the model was known/served but not open-weight.

dives.why_it_matters: Anyone who has relied on crowd-sourced human labels for training or eval data should note both that the platform's 'human' signal was already contaminated by workers using LLMs, and that the industry's shift to model-graded/synthetic data quietly removes the last cheap window onto what real, disagreeing humans actually think.

For anyone building AI education tools, the study is a corrective: the measurable exam gains came from constructed-response practice (the testing/doer effect) that LLM grading makes cheap at scale, not from the conversational tutor everyone builds and almost nobody used.

dives.story: GRPO / Dr. GRPO / DAPO group-standard-deviation identity
dives.why_it_matters: Teams doing RL post-training on reasoning models have been choosing among three methods as if they were rivals; the identity shows they are one knob (the group's reward std dev = the update size), and turns folk choices about samples-per-prompt and wasted 'silent' groups into computable closed forms.

concepts_taught: the testing effect / doer effect in learning
the group-standard-deviation identity in RLVR (why unanimous sample groups teach nothing)
